stem: Use an integrating factor to solve the linear equation \( y' + [[a]]y = [[rhs]] \) with \( y(0) = [[y0]] \). What is \( y([[t0]]) \)?
distractor_rationales: This uses \( e^{+[[a]]x} \) instead of the decaying \( e^{-[[a]]x} \).
This drops the steady-state term \( [[s]] \) and decays from \( [[y0]] \).
This reports only the equilibrium value, ignoring the transient.
This flips the sign of the transient amplitude.
This swaps the roles of the initial value and the steady state.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: Use an integrating factor to solve \( y' + [[a]]y = [[b]]x \), a linear equation with a first-degree forcing term, given \( y(0) = [[y0]] \). Find \( y([[t0]]) \).
solution: Try a particular solution \( y_p = Bx+D \); matching coefficients gives \( B=\dfrac{[[b]]}{[[a]]} \) and \( D=-\dfrac{[[b]]}{[[a]]^{2}} \). The homogeneous part is \( Ce^{-[[a]]x} \), and \( y(0)=[[y0]] \) gives \( C = [[y0]]+\dfrac{[[b]]}{[[a]]^{2}} = [[C]] \). So \( y(x) = \dfrac{[[b]]}{[[a]]}x-\dfrac{[[b]]}{[[a]]^{2}} + [[C]]e^{-[[a]]x} \), and \( y([[t0]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This drops the constant shift \( -b/a^{2} \) from the particular solution entirely.
This uses the raw initial value \( y_0 \) instead of correcting it by the particular solution's value at \( 0 \).
This forgets to divide the linear term's slope by \( [[a]] \).
This uses the wrong sign in the decaying exponential.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: Use an integrating factor to solve \( y' + [[a]]y = [[b]]e^{[[c]]x} \) (the forcing term is not itself a solution of the homogeneous equation), given \( y(0)=[[y0]] \). Find \( y([[t0]]) \).
distractor_rationales: This swaps the roles of the particular and homogeneous coefficients.
This uses the wrong sign on the forcing rate \( c \) in the particular solution.
This ignores the denominator \( (a+c) \) entirely, using \( b \) as the amplitude.
This uses \( a-c \) instead of \( a+c \) in the denominator.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: Use an integrating factor to solve \( y' + [[a]]y = [[b]]e^{-[[a]]x} \) — note the forcing term shares the homogeneous solution's exponential rate (resonance) — given \( y(0)=[[y0]] \). Find \( y([[t0]]) \).
solution: The integrating factor is \( \mu=e^{[[a]]x} \), giving \( (e^{[[a]]x}y)' = [[b]] \) (the exponentials cancel because the forcing matches the homogeneous rate). Integrating, \( e^{[[a]]x}y = [[b]]x+C \), so \( y=([[b]]x+C)e^{-[[a]]x} \). Using \( y(0)=[[y0]] \), \( C=[[y0]] \). Hence \( y([[t0]]) = ([[b]]\cdot[[t0]]+[[y0]])e^{-[[a]]\cdot[[t0]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets to multiply the forcing coefficient by \( x \), missing the resonance correction.
This uses the wrong sign in the exponential.
This mistakenly integrates \( b \) twice, picking up an extra factor of \( x^{2}/2 \).
This drops the initial value \( y_0 \) entirely.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: The variable-coefficient linear equation \( xy' + y = [[k]] \) (equivalently \( y' + \dfrac{y}{x} = \dfrac{[[k]]}{x} \), for \( x>0 \)) has integrating factor \( \mu = x \). Given \( y([[x0]]) = [[y0]] \), use it to find \( y([[t0]]) \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets to scale the constant \( C \) by \( x_0 \) when solving for it.
This swaps the roles of \( x_0 \) and \( t_0 \) in the formula.
This treats the equation as if \( a \) were a constant coefficient rather than \( 1/x \).
This uses the wrong sign on the \( C/x \) term.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: A tank initially contains \( [[Q0]] \) kg of salt dissolved in solution. Brine flows in and well-mixed solution flows out at matched rates, driving the salt amount \( Q(t) \) toward an equilibrium of \( [[s]] \) kg according to \( \dfrac{dQ}{dt} = [[a]]\left([[s]] - Q\right) \). Using an integrating factor, find \( Q([[t0]]) \) (in kg) given \( Q(0) = [[Q0]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses a growing exponential instead of the correct decaying one.
This ignores the equilibrium level entirely, decaying toward zero.
This reports only the equilibrium value, ignoring the transient from \( Q_0 \).
This flips the sign of the transient term.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
